content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 24, 2011

Steven L. Flower
Richards Watson Gershon
355 So. Grand Avenue
Los Angeles, California 90071-3101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-11-186

Dear Mr. Flower:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Rita Strickroth, a member of
the City of Seal Beach DWP Specific Plan Advisory Committee (the "Committee"), regarding
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")' Please note this letter
is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission")
does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
71.) There are other bodies of law, separate and apart from the Act's conflict-of-interest
provisions, which may apply to the situation you have described. We urge you to check with the
Attorney General's office to determine whether any other laws are applicable in light of the facts
you present.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Ms. Strickroth from participating in Committee decisions regarding
a proposed development project, if she owns a home located more than 500 feet from the project
area and has a non-exclusive easement in her community's common areas, some of which are
located less than 500 feet from the project area?

CONCLUSION

Ms. Strickroth is not a "public official," as defined in Section 82048, because the
committee does not exercise decisionmaking authority. Therefore, she is not covered by the
Act's conflict-of-interest provisions.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-11-186
Page No. 2

FACTS

Ms. Strickroth is one of seven members of the Committee. The Committee was
established by the Seal Beach City Council under the DWP Specific Plan to provide citizen input
and review of all matters relating to the Specific Plan for a proposed project. Bay City Partners
LLC has proposed to develop 10.9 acres of undeveloped land in the City of Seal Beach. The
property was formerly owned by the Los Angeles Department of Water and Power and is,
therefore, commonly referred to as the "DWP site." Development of the site is subject to the
requirements of the City of Los Angeles Department of Water and Power Specific Plan (the
"Specific Plan.")

You state that the Committee has a purely advisory role in reviewing the project. It will
review the project and recommend to the Planning Commission and City Council whether the
project should be approved, conditionally approved, or denied. The Committee will also have
the opportunity to provide comments on the Environmental Impact Report, but is not required to
make any recommendations regarding its adequacy. The Committee has no final
decisionmaking authority over any aspect of the project.

You have also stated that the Committee does not have an exclusive power to initiate a
decision nor does it have power to veto a decision, which can be overridden. Except for the
tentative tract application, for which the Planning Commission has final decisionmaking
authority, subject to appeal to the City Council, all final decisions rest with the City Council.
Committee members are not included in the city's conflict-of-interest code.

The last time the Committee made a recommendation to the Planning Commission or the
City Council was in 1996, when the DWP Specific Plan was amended. You have reviewed city
council minutes and determined that a decision made at one meeting to limit the number of
permitted hotel rooms in connection with development of the site was made independently. You
state that it is not clear whether the Planning Commission or the Committee made a
recommendation on this issue. However, you state that initially the Council considered a 300-
room limit, and after considerable debate, voted to approve a 150-room limit. Thus, regardless
of what the Committee's recommendation may have been on the issue, it was not summarily
accepted by the Council. The Committee has met only twice since then, on February 17, 2010,
and September 19, 2011. The Committee made no decisions regarding recommendations at
either meeting.

Ms. Strickroth owns a home located more than 500 feet from the project area. In
addition, she has a non-exclusive easement for ingress and egress in, to, over and through her
community's common areas, some of which are located less than 500 feet from the project area.

Ms. Strickroth wishes to participate in decisions to make recommendations regarding the
proposed project, including recommendations to amend a General Plan, DWP Specific Plan,
Zoning Map and Redevelopment Plan and to adopt a Tentative Tract Map, lot line adjustment,
public street vacation, grading permits and encroachment permits. You ask whether the Act's
conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit her from participating in these decisions.

File No. A-11-186
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.

Step 1. Is Ms. Strickroth a "public official" within the meaning of Section 87100?

The Act defines the term "public official" as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency . . . ." (Section 82048.)

The term "public official" is further defined by Regulation 18701(a)(1):

"(a) For purposes of Government Code section 82048, which defines 'public
official,' and Government Code section 82019, which defines 'designated
employee,' the following definitions apply:

(1) 'Member' shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or unsalaried
members of committees, boards or commissions with decisionmaking
authority."

Under Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A) a board or commission possesses
decisionmaking authority whenever:

"(i) It may make a final governmental decision;

(ii) It may compel a governmental decision or it may prevent a governmental
decision either by reason of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by
reason of a veto which may not be overridden; or

(iii) It makes substantive recommendations which are, and over an extended
period of time have been, regularly approved without significant amendment
or modification by another public official or governmental agency."

If the Committee meets any of the tests of Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A)(i), (ii) or (iii), it
possesses decisionmaking authority, and its members would be considered public officials who

File No. A-11-186
Page No. 4

are subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules. Alternatively, if the Committee does not have decisionmaking authority under Regulation 18701(a)(1), the Committee's members are not considered public officials under the Act and are not subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules solely by virtue of their membership on the Committee.

You state that the Committee is purely an advisory committee whose task is to provide recommendations to the Planning Commission and the City Council regarding the development project. The Committee does not have decisionmaking authority, does not make any final governmental decisions (Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A)(i).) or compel or prevent a governmental decision. (Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A)(ii).) All final decisions rest with the City Council. Any recommendations made by the Committee may be accepted or rejected by the City Council, in whole or in part. You have also indicated that Committee members' positions are not included in the City's conflict-of-interest code. Under these facts, neither clause (a)(1)(A)(i) nor clause (a)(1)(A)(ii) of Regulation 18701 apply.

Even if it is determined that a committee serves in a solely advisory function, Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A)(iii) also requires an assessment of the impact of an advisory body's recommendations by analyzing the extent to which its recommendations have been followed in the past. If there is a history of a particular advisory body's recommendations being regularly accepted without amendment or modification by the City Council, in whole or in part, the body converts from solely advisory function to one of making or participating in the making of a governmental decision, and its members are considered public officials subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions.

You indicate that the Committee has made no recommendations since 1995, when the Specific Plan was last amended. You have reviewed minutes of the City Council at that time which suggest that the Council made a final decision regarding hotel room-limits for the project independently of any recommendation that may have been made by the Committee. This conclusion is based on the fact that the discussion started with consideration of a 300-room limit, and, after considerable debate, the final decision was for a 150-room limit. The minutes do not mention any recommendation on this topic made by the Committee. However, even if the Committee had made a recommendation regarding the room-limits and that recommendation was adopted by the City Council, a single recommendation, made sixteen years ago, does not amount to substantive "recommendations" that were made "over an extended period of time."

Accordingly, the Committee does not have decisionmaking authority under Regulation 18701(a)(1), and its members are not currently considered public officials.² Thus, Ms. Strickroth is not subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules solely by virtue of her membership on the Committee.

____________________
² This conclusion is based on the facts provided. If, in the future, the Committee's substantive recommendations are regularly approved without significant amendment or modification by the City Council over an extended period of time, then the Committee members would be considered public officials subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules.

File No. A-11-186
Page No. 5


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl

located less than 500 feet from the project area?

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the 
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100 
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. 
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an 
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, 
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental 
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her 
financial interests. 
Step 1. Is Ms. Strickroth a "public official" within the meaning of Section 87100? 
The Act defines the term "public official" as "every member, officer, employee or 
consultant of a state or local government agency . ... " (Section 82048.) 
The term "public official" is further defined by Regulation 18701(a)(l): 
"(a) For purposes of Government Code section 82048, which defines 'public 
official,' and Government Code section 82019, which defines 'designated 
employee,' the following definitions apply: 
(1) 'Member' shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or unsalaried 
members of committees, boards or commissions with decisionmaking 
authority." 
Under Regulation 18701(a)(l)(A) a board or commission possesses 
decisionmaking authority whenever: 
"(i) It may make a final governmental decision; 
(ii) It may compel a governmental decision; or it may prevent a governmental 
decision either by reason of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by 
reason of a veto which may not be overridden; or 
·:. 
(iii) It makes substantive recommendations which are, and over an extended 
period of time have been, regularly approved without significant amendment 
or modification by another public official or governmental agency." 
If the Committee meets any of the tests of Regulation 18701(a)(l)(A)(i), (ii) or (iii), it 
possesses decisionmaking authority, and its members would be considered public officials who  
are subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules. Alternatively, if the Committee does not have 
decisionmaking authority under Regulation 18701(a)(l), the Committee's members are not 
considered public officials under the Act and are not subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules 
solely by virtue of their membership on the Committee. 
You state that the Committee is purely an advisory committee whose task is to provide 
recommendations to the Planning Commission and the City Council regarding the development 
project. The Committee does not have decisionmaking authority, does not make any final 
governmental decisions (Regulation 18701(a)(l)(A)(i),) or compel or prevent a governmental 
decision. (Regulation 1870l(a)(l)(A)(ii).) All final decisions rest with the City Council. Any 
recommendations made by the Committee may be accepted or rejected by the City Council, in 
whole or in part. You have also indicated that Committee members' positions are not included 
in the City's conflict-of-interest code. Under these facts, neither clause (a)(l)(A)(i) nor clause 
(a)(l)(A)(ii) of Regulation 18701 apply. 
Even if it is determined that a committee serves in a solely advisory function, Regulation 
18701(a)(l)(A)(iii) also requires an assessment of the impact of an advisory body's 
recommendations by analyzing the extent to which its recommendations have been followed in 
the past. If there is a history of a particular advisory body's recommendations being regularly 
accepted without amendment or modification, the body converts from a solely advisory function 
to one of making or participating in the making of a governmental decision, and its members are 
considered public officials subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions. 
You indicate that the Committee has made no recommendations since 1995, when the 
Specific Plan was last amended. You have reviewed minutes of the City Council at that time 
which suggest that the Council made a final decision regarding hotel room-limits for the project 
independently of any recommendation that may have been made by the Committee. This 
conclusion is based on the fact that the discussion started with consideration of a 300-room limit, 
and, after considerable debate, the final decision was for a 150-room limit. The minutes do not 
mention any recommendation on this topic made by the Committee. However, even if the 
Committee had made a recommendation regarding the room-limits and that recommendation was 
adopted by the City Council, a single recommendation, made sixteen years ago, does not amount 
to substantive "recommendations" that were made "over an extended period of time." 
Accordingly, the Committee does not have decisionmaking authority under Regulation 
18701(a)(l), and its members are not currently considered public officials.2 Thus, Ms. Strickroth 
is not subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules solely by virtue of her membership on the 
Committee. 
2 This conclusion is based on the facts provided. If, in the future, the Committee's substantive 
recommendations are regularly approved without significant amendment or modification by the City Council over 
an extended period of time, then the Committee members would be considered public officials subject to the Act's 
conflict-of-interest rules.
